---
title: "Cameron & Trivedi -- Ch. 5: GLS regression (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Colin Cameron
- Pravin Trivedi
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Translation of *Microeconometrics Using Stata* Chapter 5, routed through
[``StatEcon``](https://github.com/ElvisCasco/StatEcon). Chapter 5 covers GLS
and FGLS estimation, heteroskedastic regression, SUR (Seemingly Unrelated
Regressions), and survey-weighted / cluster-robust inference. Three new
StatEcon helper families are introduced here: ``stata_sureg`` /
``stata_sureg_fit`` / ``stata_test_sureg`` for the SUR family;
``stata_mean`` / ``stata_svy_mean`` / ``stata_svy_regress`` for survey
estimation; and ``stata_regress_cluster`` for weighted OLS with one-way
cluster-robust standard errors.

[Microeconometrics with R](https://ycroissant.github.io/micsr_book/)
- [UCLA Stata modules](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/modules/)
- [Github](https://github.com/thousandoaks?tab=repositories)

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
const DATA = first(filter(d -> isdir(d) && (isfile(joinpath(d, "auto.dta")) || isdir(joinpath(d, "musr"))), ["../../data", "data", raw"C:/EC/GitHub/StatEcon/data"]))
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq
```

# 5 GLS regression

## 5.1 Introduction

## 5.2 GLS and FGLS regression

### 5.2.1 GLS for heteroskedastic errors

### 5.2.2 GLS and FGLS

### 5.2.3 Weighted least squares and robust standard errors

### 5.2.4 Leading examples

## 5.3 Modeling heteroskedastic data

### 5.3.1 Simulated dataset

The DGP is: `y = 1 + 1·x2 + 1·x3 + u` where `u = sqrt(exp(-1 + 0.2·x2))·e`, with `x2, x3, e ~ N(0, 5²)`. Errors are heteroskedastic with `V[u|x] = exp(-1 + 0.2·x2)`.

```{julia}
#=
* This uses generated data
* Model is  y = 1 + 1*x2 + 1*x3 + u
* where     u = sqrt(exp(-1+0.2*x2))*e
*           x1 ~ N(0, 5^2)
*           x2 ~ N(0, 5^2)
*           e ~ N(0, 5^2)
* Errors are conditionally heteroskedastic with V[u|x]=exp(-1+1*x2)
=#
# Generated data for heteroskedasticity example
# Read from CSV exported by Stata (ensures identical data across both notebooks)
df5 = CSV.read("$(DATA)/musr/5_3_1.csv", DataFrame)
for c in names(df5)
    df5[!, c] = Float64.(df5[!, c])
end
DataFrames.describe(df5)
```

```{julia}
#=
clear all
* Generated data for heteroskedasticity example
set seed 10101
quietly set obs 500
generate double x2 = 5*rnormal(0)
generate double x3 = 5*rnormal(0)
generate double e  = 5*rnormal(0)
generate double u  = sqrt(exp(-1+0.2*x2))*e
generate double y  = 1 + 1*x2 + 1*x3 + u
summarize
export delimited using "musr/5_3_1.csv", replace
=#
stata_summarize(df5)
```

### 5.3.2 OLS estimation

```{julia}
#=
* OLS regression with default standard errors
regress y x2 x3
=#
# OLS with default SE
ols5 = FixedEffectModels.reg(df5, @formula(y ~ x2 + x3))
```

```{julia}
#=
* OLS regression with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors
regress y x2 x3, vce(robust) 
=#
#* OLS regression with default standard errors
#regress y x2 x3
stata_regress(df5, :y, [:x2, :x3])
```

```{julia}
# OLS with heteroskedasticity-robust SE
ols5r = FixedEffectModels.reg(df5, @formula(y ~ x2 + x3), Vcov.robust())
```

```{julia}
#* OLS regression with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors
#regress y x2 x3, vce(robust) 
stata_regress(df5, :y, [:x2, :x3]; vcov=Vcov.robust())
```

### 5.3.3 Detecting heteroskedasticity

```{julia}
#=
* Heteroskedasticity diagnostic scatterplot
quietly regress y x2 x3
predict double uhat, resid
generate double absu = abs(uhat)
quietly twoway (scatter absu x2) (lowess absu x2, bw(0.4) lw(thick)), ///
  scale(1.2) xscale(titleg(*5)) yscale(titleg(*5))                    ///
  plotr(style(none)) name(gls1)
quietly twoway (scatter absu x3) (lowess absu x3, bw(0.4) lw(thick)), ///
  scale(1.2) xscale(titleg(*5)) yscale(titleg(*5))                    ///
  plotr(style(none)) name(gls2)
graph combine gls1 gls2
=#
# Heteroskedasticity diagnostic scatterplots
yhat5 = FixedEffectModels.predict(ols5, df5)
df5.uhat = df5.y .- yhat5
df5.absu = abs.(df5.uhat)

# Lowess smoother via Loess.jl
lo2 = Loess.loess(df5.x2, df5.absu; span = 0.4)
lo3 = Loess.loess(df5.x3, df5.absu; span = 0.4)

fig5 = Figure(size = (900, 400))
ax1 = Axis(fig5[1, 1], xlabel = "x2", ylabel = "|uhat|")
scatter!(ax1, df5.x2, df5.absu, markersize = 4)
xs2 = sort(df5.x2)
lines!(ax1, xs2, Loess.predict(lo2, xs2), color = :red, linewidth = 2)

ax2 = Axis(fig5[1, 2], xlabel = "x3", ylabel = "|uhat|")
scatter!(ax2, df5.x3, df5.absu, markersize = 4)
xs3 = sort(df5.x3)
lines!(ax2, xs3, Loess.predict(lo3, xs3), color = :red, linewidth = 2)
fig5
```

```{julia}
#=
graph export musr/mus05gls_fig1.eps, replace
drop uhat
=#
# Breusch–Pagan test of heteroskedasticity (regress uhat² on regressors)
df5.uhat2 = df5.uhat.^2
m_bp5 = FixedEffectModels.reg(df5, @formula(uhat2 ~ x2 + x3))
LM = nrow(df5) * StatsBase.r2(m_bp5)
@printf("BP LM(2) = %.4f, p = %.4f\n", LM, 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), LM))
```

```{julia}
#=
* Test heteroskedasticity depending on x2, x3, and x2 and x3
estat hettest x2 x3, mtest
=#
# estat hettest x2 x3, mtest  (individual + joint tests with Bonferroni/Sidak corrections)
p_individual = Float64[]
for v in (:x2, :x3)
    m = FixedEffectModels.reg(df5, term(:uhat2) ~ term(v))
    LM = nrow(df5) * StatsBase.r2(m)
    p = 1 - cdf(Chisq(1), LM)
    push!(p_individual, p)
    @printf("BP test on %s only: LM(1) = %.4f, p = %.4f\n", v, LM, p)
end
# Multiple-testing corrections (mtest)
ntests = length(p_individual)
for (i, (vname, p)) in enumerate(zip(["x2", "x3"], p_individual))
    p_bonf = min(p * ntests, 1.0)
    p_sidak = 1 - (1 - p)^ntests
    @printf("  %s: Bonferroni p = %.4f, Šidák p = %.4f\n", vname, p_bonf, p_sidak)
end
# Joint test
m_joint = FixedEffectModels.reg(df5, @formula(uhat2 ~ x2 + x3))
LM_joint = nrow(df5) * StatsBase.r2(m_joint)
@printf("BP joint test on x2 x3: LM(2) = %.4f, p = %.4f\n",
        LM_joint, 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), LM_joint))
```

```{julia}
#=
* Not included in book
* Separate tests of heteroskedasticity using iid version of hettest
estat hettest x2, iid
estat hettest x3, iid
estat hettest x2 x3, iid
=#
# estat hettest x2 x3, mtest
estat_hettest(ols5, df5; vars=[:x2, :x3], mtest=true)
```

```{julia}
# Not included in book
# Separate tests of heteroskedasticity using iid version of hettest
estat_hettest(ols5, df5; vars=[:x2], iid=true)
estat_hettest(ols5, df5; vars=[:x3], iid=true)
estat_hettest(ols5, df5; vars=[:x2, :x3], iid=true)
```

### 5.3.4 FGLS estimation

```{julia}
#=
clear all
* Generated data for heteroskedasticity example
set seed 10101
quietly set obs 500
generate double x2 = 5*rnormal(0)
generate double x3 = 5*rnormal(0)
generate double e  = 5*rnormal(0)
generate double u  = sqrt(exp(-1+0.2*x2))*e
generate double y  = 1 + 1*x2 + 1*x3 + u
export delimited using "musr/5_3_1.csv", replace

* FGLS: First step get estimate of skedasticity function
quietly regress y x2 x3                  // get bols
predict double uhat, resid
generate double uhatsq = uhat^2          // get squared residual 
generate double one = 1 
* NLS of uhatsq on exp(z'a)                      
capture nl (uhatsq = exp({xb: x2 one})), nolog   
=#
# FGLS step 1: estimate skedasticity function via NLS:  σ²(x) = exp(α0 + α1·x2)
function neg_lik(α, x, y)
    pred = exp.(α[1] .+ α[2] .* x)
    sum((y .- pred).^2)
end
res_α = optimize(α -> neg_lik(α, df5.x2, df5.uhat2), [0.0, 0.0])
α_hat = Optim.minimizer(res_α)
df5.varu = exp.(α_hat[1] .+ α_hat[2] .* df5.x2)
@printf("α̂ = (%.4f, %.4f)\n", α_hat[1], α_hat[2])
```

```{julia}
#=
*get sigmahat^2
predict double varu, yhat               
=#
# FGLS step 2: weighted regression with weights 1/varu
df5.w_fgls = 1 ./ df5.varu
fgls5 = FixedEffectModels.reg(df5, @formula(y ~ x2 + x3), weights = :w_fgls)
```

```{julia}
#=
* FGLS: Second step get estimate of skedasticity function
regress y x2 x3 [aweight=1/varu] 
=#
# FGLS step 2: weighted regression with weights 1/varu
stata_regress(df5, :y, [:x2, :x3]; weights = :w_fgls)
```

### 5.3.5 WLS estimation

```{julia}
#=
* WLS estimator is FGLS with robust estimate of VCE
regress y x2 x3 [aweight=1/varu], vce(robust)
=#
# WLS = FGLS with robust SE
wls5 = FixedEffectModels.reg(df5, @formula(y ~ x2 + x3), Vcov.robust(), weights = :w_fgls)
```

```{julia}
#=
* FGLS: First step get estimate of skedasticity function
quietly regress y x2 x3 if _n < 451
quietly predict double yhat1
quietly regress y x2 x3 [aweight=1/varu] if _n < 451
quietly predict double yhat2
generate double perr1 = y - yhat1
generate double perr2 = y - yhat2
summarize y yhat1 yhat2 perr1 perr2 if _n > 450
=#
# WLS = FGLS with robust SE
wls5 = stata_regress(df5, :y, [:x2, :x3]; weights = :w_fgls, vcov=Vcov.robust())
```

```{julia}
# Prediction comparison: OLS vs WLS on first 450 obs, evaluate on last 50
df_train = df5[1:450, :]
ols_sub  = FixedEffectModels.reg(df_train, @formula(y ~ x2 + x3))
wls_sub  = FixedEffectModels.reg(df_train, @formula(y ~ x2 + x3), weights = :w_fgls)

yhat1 = StatsBase.coef(ols_sub)[1] .+ StatsBase.coef(ols_sub)[2] .* df5.x2 .+ StatsBase.coef(ols_sub)[3] .* df5.x3
yhat2 = StatsBase.coef(wls_sub)[1] .+ StatsBase.coef(wls_sub)[2] .* df5.x2 .+ StatsBase.coef(wls_sub)[3] .* df5.x3
perr1 = df5.y .- yhat1
perr2 = df5.y .- yhat2

test_df = DataFrame(y = df5.y[451:end],
                    yhat_ols = yhat1[451:end], yhat_wls = yhat2[451:end],
                    err_ols  = perr1[451:end], err_wls  = perr2[451:end])
DataFrames.describe(test_df)
```

```{julia}
stata_summarize(test_df)
```

## 5.4 System of linear regressions

### 5.4.1 SUR model

### 5.4.2 The sureg command

### 5.4.3 Application to two categories of expenditures

```{julia}
#=
* Summary statistics for seemingly unrelated regressions example
clear all
use musr/mus05surdata.dta
summarize ldrugexp ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr medicaid private
=#
# Summary statistics for seemingly unrelated regressions example
df_sur = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus05surdata.dta"))
DataFrames.describe(df_sur[:, [:ldrugexp, :ltotothr, :age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr, :medicaid, :private]])
```

```{julia}
#=
* Not included in book
summarize ldrugexp if ldrugexp!=. & ltotothr!=.
=#
stata_summarize(df_sur, [:ldrugexp, :ltotothr, :age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr, :medicaid, :private])
```

```{julia}
#=
* SUR estimation of a seemingly unrelated regressions model
sureg (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private) ///
  (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private), corr
=#
# Not included in book
sub = dropmissing(df_sur, [:ldrugexp, :ltotothr])
DataFrames.describe(sub[:, [:ldrugexp]])
```

```{julia}
stata_summarize(sub, [:ldrugexp])
```

```{julia}
# Not included in book
stata_summarize(sub)
```


```{julia}
# SUR estimation (FGLS) — sureg (ldrugexp ...) (ltotothr ...), corr
# Step 1: equation-by-equation OLS to get Σ̂; Step 2: FGLS using Σ⁻¹ ⊗ I_N
sur_data = dropmissing(df_sur, [:ldrugexp, :ltotothr, :age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr, :medicaid, :private])
Ns = nrow(sur_data)

# Design matrices (equation-specific regressors)
X1 = hcat(ones(Ns), sur_data.age, sur_data.age2, sur_data.actlim,
          sur_data.totchr, sur_data.medicaid, sur_data.private)
X2 = hcat(ones(Ns), sur_data.age, sur_data.age2, sur_data.educyr,
          sur_data.actlim, sur_data.totchr, sur_data.private)
Y1 = Float64.(sur_data.ldrugexp)
Y2 = Float64.(sur_data.ltotothr)
cn1 = ["_cons", "age", "age2", "actlim", "totchr", "medicaid", "private"]
cn2 = ["_cons", "age", "age2", "educyr", "actlim", "totchr", "private"]

# Step 1: OLS residuals
β1_ols = X1 \ Y1;  e1 = Y1 - X1 * β1_ols
β2_ols = X2 \ Y2;  e2 = Y2 - X2 * β2_ols
Σ̂ = [e1'e1 e1'e2; e2'e1 e2'e2] ./ Ns

# Step 2: FGLS (SUR)
Σinv = LinearAlgebra.inv(Σ̂)
# Block structure: X = blockdiag(X1, X2), W = Σ⁻¹ ⊗ I_N
XWX = Σinv[1,1]*(X1'X1) + zeros(size(X1,2), size(X2,2))
XWX = [Σinv[1,1]*(X1'X1)  Σinv[1,2]*(X1'X2);
       Σinv[2,1]*(X2'X1)  Σinv[2,2]*(X2'X2)]
XWY = [Σinv[1,1]*(X1'Y1) + Σinv[1,2]*(X1'Y2);
       Σinv[2,1]*(X2'Y1) + Σinv[2,2]*(X2'Y2)]
β_sur = XWX \ XWY
V_sur = LinearAlgebra.inv(XWX)
se_sur = sqrt.(max.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_sur), 0.0))

k1 = size(X1, 2); k2 = size(X2, 2)
println("=== SUR (FGLS) Equation 1: ldrugexp ===")
println(DataFrame(variable=cn1, Coef=β_sur[1:k1], SE=se_sur[1:k1],
              z=β_sur[1:k1]./se_sur[1:k1]))
println("\n=== SUR (FGLS) Equation 2: ltotothr ===")
println(DataFrame(variable=cn2, Coef=β_sur[k1+1:end], SE=se_sur[k1+1:end],
              z=β_sur[k1+1:end]./se_sur[k1+1:end]))

# Cross-equation residual correlation (corr option)
rho = Σ̂[1,2] / sqrt(Σ̂[1,1] * Σ̂[2,2])
@printf("\nCorrelation matrix of residuals:\n  ldrugexp  ltotothr\n  1.0000    %.4f\n  %.4f    1.0000\n", rho, rho)
@printf("Breusch-Pagan test of independence: chi2(1) = %.3f, Pr = %.4f\n",
        Ns * rho^2, 1 - cdf(Chisq(1), Ns * rho^2))

# Save OLS-based models for later use (bootstrap, testparm)
eq1 = FixedEffectModels.reg(sur_data, @formula(ldrugexp ~ age + age2 + actlim + totchr + medicaid + private))
eq2 = FixedEffectModels.reg(sur_data, @formula(ltotothr ~ age + age2 + educyr + actlim + totchr + private))
```

```{julia}
#* SUR estimation of a seemingly unrelated regressions model
#sureg (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private) ///
#  (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private), corr
stata_sureg(df_sur,
      :ldrugexp => [:age, :age2, :actlim, :totchr, :medicaid, :private],
      :ltotothr => [:age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr, :private])
```

### 5.4.4 Robust standard errors

```{julia}
#=
* Bootstrap to get heteroskedasticity-robust SEs for SUR estimator 
bootstrap, reps(400) seed(10101) nodots: sureg        ///
  (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private)  ///
  (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private) 
=#
# Bootstrap to obtain SEs for the two SUR equations (B = 400)
Random.seed!(10101)
B = 400
β1_boot = Vector{Vector{Float64}}(undef, B)
β2_boot = Vector{Vector{Float64}}(undef, B)
for b in 1:B
    idx = sample(1:nrow(sur_data), nrow(sur_data); replace = true)
    sub = sur_data[idx, :]
    m1 = FixedEffectModels.reg(sub, @formula(ldrugexp ~ age + age2 + actlim + totchr + medicaid + private))
    m2 = FixedEffectModels.reg(sub, @formula(ltotothr ~ age + age2 + educyr + actlim + totchr + private))
    β1_boot[b] = StatsBase.coef(m1)
    β2_boot[b] = StatsBase.coef(m2)
end
β1_mat = reduce(hcat, β1_boot)'
β2_mat = reduce(hcat, β2_boot)'
println("Eq1 bootstrap SEs: ", round.(std(β1_mat, dims = 1), digits = 4))
println("Eq2 bootstrap SEs: ", round.(std(β2_mat, dims = 1), digits = 4))
```

```{julia}
#* Bootstrap to get heteroskedasticity-robust SEs for SUR estimator
#bootstrap, reps(400) seed(10101) nodots: sureg        ///
#  (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private)  ///
#  (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private)
stata_sureg(df_sur,
      :ldrugexp => [:age, :age2, :actlim, :totchr, :medicaid, :private],
      :ltotothr => [:age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr, :private];
      reps=400, seed=10101)
```

### 5.4.5 Testing cross-equation constraints

```{julia}
#=
* Test of variables in both equations
quietly sureg (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private) ///
   (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private)
test age age2
=#
# Joint Wald test of age and age2 in equation 1
nm = StatsBase.coefnames(eq1)
idx = [findfirst(==(n), nm) for n in ("age", "age2")]
βt  = StatsBase.coef(eq1)[idx]; Vt = StatsBase.vcov(eq1)[idx, idx]
W = try
    βt' * LinearAlgebra.inv(Vt) * βt
catch
    NaN
end
@printf("W(2) = %.4f, p = %.4f\n", W, 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), W))
```

```{julia}
#=
* Test of variables in just the first equation
test [ldrugexp]age [ldrugexp]age2
=#
# Test of age and age2 in equation 1 only (same as above)
println("Same Wald test on equation 1; see above.")
```

```{julia}
#=
* Test of a restriction across the two equations
test [ldrugexp]private = [ltotothr]private
=#
# Cross-equation restriction:  β_private (eq1) = β_private (eq2)
# Test using the bootstrap distribution
β1_priv = β1_mat[:, findfirst(==("private"), StatsBase.coefnames(eq1))]
β2_priv = β2_mat[:, findfirst(==("private"), StatsBase.coefnames(eq2))]
diff_b  = β1_priv .- β2_priv
@printf("mean(diff) = %.4f  bootstrap SE = %.4f  z = %.3f\n",
        mean(diff_b), std(diff_b), mean(diff_b) / std(diff_b))
```

```{julia}
# sureg (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private) ///
#       (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private)
# test age age2
#
# Joint test of age and age2 across BOTH equations (4 restrictions).
# Uses the bootstrap draws computed earlier (β1_mat, β2_mat) to estimate the
# joint covariance — equation-by-equation OLS vcov misses the cross-equation
# correlation that Stata's sureg accounts for via FGLS.
i1 = [findfirst(==(n), StatsBase.coefnames(eq1)) for n in ("age", "age2")]
i2 = [findfirst(==(n), StatsBase.coefnames(eq2)) for n in ("age", "age2")]

β_joint = vcat(StatsBase.coef(eq1)[i1], StatsBase.coef(eq2)[i2])
B_joint = hcat(β1_mat[:, i1], β2_mat[:, i2])      # B × 4 bootstrap draws
V_joint = Statistics.cov(B_joint)

W = β_joint' * (V_joint \ β_joint)
q = length(β_joint)
p = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(q), W)
Printf.@printf("Joint Wald test: H0 : age = age2 = 0 in both equations\n")
Printf.@printf("  chi2(%d) = %.4f   p = %.4f\n", q, W, p)
```

```{julia}
#* Test of variables in both equations
#quietly sureg (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private) ///
#   (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private)
#test age age2
fit_sur = stata_sureg_fit(df_sur,
                    :ldrugexp => [:age, :age2, :actlim, :totchr, :medicaid, :private],
                    :ltotothr => [:age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr, :private])
stata_test_sureg(fit_sur, [:age, :age2]);
```

```{julia}
#* Test of variables in just the first equation
#test [ldrugexp]age [ldrugexp]age2
stata_test_sureg(fit_sur, ["[ldrugexp]age", "[ldrugexp]age2"]);
```

```{julia}
#* Test of a restriction across the two equations
#test [ldrugexp]private = [ltotothr]private
stata_test_sureg(fit_sur, ["[ldrugexp]private = [ltotothr]private"]);
```

### 5.4.6 Imposing cross-equation constraints

```{julia}
#=
* Specify a restriction across the two equations
constraint 1 [ldrugexp]private = [ltotothr]private
=#
# constraint 1 [ldrugexp]private = [ltotothr]private
# sureg (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private) ///
#       (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private), constraints(1)
#
# Strategy: stack the two equations into a single regression where `private`
# appears as ONE shared regressor (its coefficient is the constrained value).
# All other regressors are "padded" with zeros in the other equation's block.
# A first OLS pass on the stacked system gives a consistent restricted
# estimate; a second FGLS pass uses Σ̂ from those residuals for efficiency,
# matching what Stata's `sureg ... , constraints()` does.

N = DataFrames.nrow(sur_data)
pad1(v) = vcat(Float64.(v), zeros(N))
pad2(v) = vcat(zeros(N), Float64.(v))

Y  = vcat(Float64.(sur_data.ldrugexp), Float64.(sur_data.ltotothr))
X  = hcat(
    pad1(ones(N)),              # ldrugexp: intercept
    pad1(sur_data.age),
    pad1(sur_data.age2),
    pad1(sur_data.actlim),
    pad1(sur_data.totchr),
    pad1(sur_data.medicaid),
    pad2(ones(N)),              # ltotothr: intercept
    pad2(sur_data.age),
    pad2(sur_data.age2),
    pad2(sur_data.educyr),
    pad2(sur_data.actlim),
    pad2(sur_data.totchr),
    vcat(Float64.(sur_data.private), Float64.(sur_data.private))   # SHARED
)
cnames = ["eq1:_cons", "eq1:age", "eq1:age2", "eq1:actlim", "eq1:totchr", "eq1:medicaid",
          "eq2:_cons", "eq2:age", "eq2:age2", "eq2:educyr", "eq2:actlim", "eq2:totchr",
          "private (shared)"]
k = size(X, 2)

# --- First pass: OLS on the stacked system (restricted) ---
β_ols = X \ Y
e     = reshape(Y - X*β_ols, N, 2)            # per-observation residuals for the 2 equations

# --- Second pass: FGLS using Σ̂ from first-pass residuals ---
Σ̂    = (e' * e) / N                            # 2×2 cross-equation covariance
Σinv = LinearAlgebra.inv(Σ̂)
# Weight matrix W = Σ⁻¹ ⊗ I_N: efficient SUR moments are  X' W Y  and  X' W X
W_lhs = Σinv[1,1]*(X[1:N,     :]'X[1:N,     :]) +
        Σinv[2,2]*(X[N+1:end, :]'X[N+1:end, :]) +
        Σinv[1,2]*(X[1:N,     :]'X[N+1:end, :]) +
        Σinv[2,1]*(X[N+1:end, :]'X[1:N,     :])
W_rhs = Σinv[1,1]*(X[1:N,     :]'Y[1:N])      +
        Σinv[2,2]*(X[N+1:end, :]'Y[N+1:end]) +
        Σinv[1,2]*(X[1:N,     :]'Y[N+1:end]) +
        Σinv[2,1]*(X[N+1:end, :]'Y[1:N])
β_fgls = W_lhs \ W_rhs
V_fgls = LinearAlgebra.inv(W_lhs)              # joint FGLS vcov
se     = sqrt.(max.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_fgls), 0.0))
z      = β_fgls ./ se
pval   = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z)))

println("FGLS (SUR) with cross-equation constraint  [ldrugexp]private = [ltotothr]private")
println("Residual cov matrix Σ̂ =")
display(Σ̂)
println("\nCoefficients:")
println(DataFrames.DataFrame(variable=cnames, estimate=β_fgls, stderr=se, z=z, p=pval))
```

```{julia}
#=
* Estimate subject to the cross-equation constraint
sureg (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private)        ///
  (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private), constraints(1)
=#
#* Specify a restriction across the two equations
#constraint 1 [ldrugexp]private = [ltotothr]private

#* Estimate subject to the cross-equation constraint
#sureg (ldrugexp age age2 actlim totchr medicaid private)        ///
#  (ltotothr age age2 educyr actlim totchr private), constraints(1)
stata_sureg(df_sur,
      :ldrugexp => [:age, :age2, :actlim, :totchr, :medicaid, :private],
      :ltotothr => [:age, :age2, :educyr, :actlim, :totchr, :private];
      constraints = ["[ldrugexp]private = [ltotothr]private"])
```

## 5.5 Survey data: Weighting, clustering, and stratification

### 5.5.1 Survey design

```{julia}
#=
* Data from http://www.stata-press.com/data/r10/nhanes2.dta

* Survey data example: NHANES II data
clear all
use musr/mus05nhanes2.dta
quietly keep if age >= 21 & age <= 65
describe sampl finalwgt strata psu
summarize sampl finalwgt strata psu
=#
# NHANES II data
df_nh = DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus05nhanes2.dta"))
df_nh = filter(r -> 21 <= r.age <= 65, df_nh)
@printf("N = %d\n", nrow(df_nh))
DataFrames.describe(df_nh[:, [:sampl, :finalwgt, :strata, :psu]])
```


```{julia}
#=
* Declare survey design
svyset psu [pweight=finalwgt], strata(strata)
=#
stata_summarize(df_nh[:, [:sampl, :finalwgt, :strata, :psu]])
```

```{julia}
#=
* Describe the survey design
svydescribe
=#
# Stata's svyset is descriptive — record the design columns for later use
println("Survey design: PSU = :psu, strata = :strata, weight = :finalwgt")
```

```{julia}
# Describe the survey design (counts of strata and PSUs)
@printf("Number of strata = %d\n", length(unique(df_nh.strata)))
@printf("Number of PSUs   = %d\n",
        length(unique(zip(df_nh.strata, df_nh.psu) |> collect)))
```

### 5.5.2 Survey mean estimation

```{julia}
#=
* Estimate the population mean using svy:
svy: mean hgb
=#
# Population mean of hgb using sampling weights
let df = dropmissing(df_nh[:, [:hgb, :finalwgt]])
    @printf("Weighted mean of hgb = %.4f\n",
            sum(df.hgb .* df.finalwgt) / sum(df.finalwgt))
end
```

```{julia}
#=
* Estimate the population mean using no weights and no cluster
mean hgb
=#
# Unweighted mean of hgb
@printf("Unweighted mean of hgb = %.4f\n",
        mean(skipmissing(df_nh.hgb)))
```


```{julia}
#* Estimate the population mean using svy:
#svy: mean hgb
stata_svy_mean(df_nh, :hgb; stratum=:strata, psu=:psu, weight=:finalwgt);
```

```{julia}
#* Estimate the population mean using no weights and no cluster
#mean hgb
stata_mean(df_nh, :hgb);
```

### 5.5.3 Survey linear regression

```{julia}
#=
* Regression using svy:
svy: regress hgb age female
=#
# Regression with weights (no cluster correction)
m_svy = FixedEffectModels.reg(df_nh, @formula(hgb ~ age + female),
            weights = :finalwgt)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Regression using weights and cluster on PSU
generate uniqpsu = 2*strata + psu  // make unique identifier for each psu
regress hgb age female [pweight=finalwgt], vce(cluster uniqpsu)
=#
# Regression with weights + clustering on unique PSU = 2·strata + psu
df_nh.uniqpsu = 2 .* df_nh.strata .+ df_nh.psu
reg(df_nh, @formula(hgb ~ age + female),
    weights = :finalwgt, Vcov.cluster(:uniqpsu))
```

```{julia}
#=
* Regression using no weights and no cluster
regress hgb age female
=#
# Regression with no weights, no cluster
reg(df_nh, @formula(hgb ~ age + female))
```


```{julia}
#* Regression using svy:
#svy: regress hgb age female
stata_svy_regress(df_nh, :hgb, [:age, :female];
            stratum=:strata, psu=:psu, weight=:finalwgt);
```


```{julia}
#* Regression using weights and cluster on PSU
#generate uniqpsu = 2*strata + psu  // make unique identifier for each psu
#regress hgb age female [pweight=finalwgt], vce(cluster uniqpsu)
df_nh.uniqpsu = 2 .* df_nh.strata .+ df_nh.psu
stata_regress_cluster(df_nh, :hgb, [:age, :female];
                cluster=:uniqpsu, weight=:finalwgt);
```

```{julia}
#* Regression using no weights and no cluster
#regress hgb age female
stata_regress(df_nh, :hgb, [:age, :female]);
```

## 5.6 Stata resources

## 5.7 Exercises
